issue_title: Use empty string for formatting abbreviated dimensionless units
issue_body:
Hi,

Currently (using git checkout  2c67bba...) the formatting of dimensionless units always yields "dimensionless" (both with and without the abbreviated format specifier "~"). I think it would be nice to return an empty string when the abbreviated format of a dimensionless unit is requested.

With an example:
~~~~
"{}".format(Quantity(1))   -->  '1 dimensionless'
"{:~}".format(Quantity(1))  --> '1 '
~~~~

If you think that this is a good suggestion, I would be happy to do a pull request.

An implementation that works for me (maybe not the optimal one) is modifying `_Unit.__format__` with an early return in the case of abbreviated dimensionless units:

~~~~
    def __format__(self, spec):
        spec = spec or self.default_format

        if '~' in spec:
            if self.dimensionless:
                return ''
            units = UnitsContainer(dict((self._REGISTRY._get_symbol(key),
                                         value)
                                   for key, value in self._units.items()))
            spec = spec.replace('~', '')
        else:
            units = self._units

        return '%s' % (format(units, spec))
~~~~

issue_title: Feature Request: Error object compatibility
issue_body:
When working with Python 2.7 and 3.6, I found that the object that is returned from an error being caught has changed.

For example:
```
try:
   import nonexistent_module
except ImportError as e:
   print(dir(e))
```
The outputs are:
Python 2.7
```
['__class__', '__delattr__', '__dict__', '__doc__', '__format__', '__getattribute__', '__getitem__', '__getslice__', '__hash__', '__init__', '__new__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__setattr__', '__setstate__', '__sizeof__', '__str__', '__subclasshook__', '__unicode__', 'args', 'message']
```
Python 3.6
```
['__cause__', '__class__', '__context__', '__delattr__', '__dict__', '__dir__', '__doc__', '__eq__', '__format__', '__ge__', '__getattribute__', '__gt__', '__hash__', '__init__', '__init_subclass__', '__le__', '__lt__', '__ne__', '__new__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__setattr__', '__setstate__', '__sizeof__', '__str__', '__subclasshook__', '__suppress_context__', '__traceback__', 'args', 'msg', 'name', 'path', 'with_traceback']
```

As can be seen, some stuff has changed.
Python 2.7
```
>>> e
>>> ImportError('No module named nonexistent_module')
>>> type(e)
>>> <type 'exceptions.ImportError'>
>>> e.args
>>> ('No module named nonexistent_module',)
>>> e.message
>>> 'No module named nonexistent_module' #  With a DeprecationWarning
```

Python 3.6
```
>>> e
>>> ModuleNotFoundError("No module named 'nonexistent_module'",)
>>> type(e)
>>> <class 'ModuleNotFoundError'>
>>> e.args
>>> ("No module named 'nonexistent_module'",)
>>> e.msg
>>> "No module named 'nonexistent_module'"
```
It would be nice if `six` could provide this object model compatibility.

why the variable in __init__() func works in this way?
KeyError: ber_class 2, ber_type 3
issue_body:
I have an issue when querying an Active Directory server.
Older ldap3 versions works, bisection shows the issue appearing between ldap3 0.9.8.8 and 0.9.9.

When the query hits an user located in a another domain, ldap3 hits a KeyError exception.
```text
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\core\connection.py", line 614, in search
    response = self.post_send_search(self.send('searchRequest', request, controls))
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\sync.py", line 142, in post_send_search
    responses, result = self.get_response(message_id)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\base.py", line 301, in get_response
    responses = self._get_response(message_id)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\strategy\sync.py", line 167, in _get_response
    ldap_resp = decode_message_fast(response)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 72, in decode_message_fast
    decoded = decode_sequence(message, ber_value_offset, ber_len + ber_value_offset, LDAP_MESSAGE_CONTEXT)
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 92, in decode_sequence
    value = ber_decoder(message, start, start + ber_len, context_decoders)  # call value decode function
  File "C:\Python34\lib\site-packages\ldap3\utils\asn1.py", line 94, in decode_sequence
    value = context_decoders[ber_type](message, start, start + ber_len)  # call value decode function for context class
KeyError: 3
```
I could make it work again by simply decoding the message using decode_octet_string.
Anonymized, the message looks like this:
```python
server == "local.domain.name"

ber_class == 2
ber_type == 3
value == b'\x04oldaps://other.domain.name/OU=Users,DC=other,DC=domain,DC=name'
```


```patch
--- a/ldap3/utils/asn1.py
+++ b/ldap3/utils/asn1.py
@@ -90,8 +90,10 @@ def decode_sequence(message, start, stop, context_decoders=None):
         start += ber_value_offset
         if ber_decoder:
             value = ber_decoder(message, start, start + ber_len, context_decoders)  # call value decode function
-        else:
+        elif ber_type in context_decoders:
             value = context_decoders[ber_type](message, start, start + ber_len)  # call value decode function for context class
+        else:
+            value = decode_octet_string(message, start, start + ber_len)
         decoded.append((ber_class, ber_constructed, ber_type, value))
         start += ber_len
 
```

issue_title: Expose a function for parsing a JWT without any verification
issue_body:
In my code, I'm implementing decoding a token that's been signed by one of the keys in a JWK Set. I'm going to aproach this according to the second option I described [here](https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/issues/144#issuecomment-328619194). I found that all I need to get the header containing the key is to call this:
```python
import jwt
token = ...
payload, signing_input, header, signature = jwt.PyJWS()._load(token)
```

But this uses a non-public API, which is far from ideal.

I've noticed, that the `_load()` method doesn't actually use `self`, so it could be made into a static method, or a standalone function. What I'm proposing is to cut the implementation out from the class and turn it into a public function. It could be called `parse_token`, or something. It'd also be cool if it returned a named tuple. What do you think?

Also, I think the code could generally use more pure functions and composition over inheritance approach.

issue_title: Autoformat of boolean equality comparison to True/False could change semantics
issue_body:
Hi! Apologies if this is not the correct place for this discussion, but I wanted to inform you about an issue we (https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore) had recently, related to autopep8, that you might want to take into consideration. It is not _necessarily_ a bug.

autopep8 will simplify boolean equality comparisons to a True/False literal, e.g.

`var == True` will be formatted to just `var`. However, if `var` is an object which overloads the `==` operator, this autoformat could result in changed semantics for the autoformatted program, depending on the `__eq__` overloading function.

Specifically for our use case, we use this style of operator overloading to conveniently create AST-like expression tree data structures. For example, `var == True`, would return an `EqualExpression` object. With the autoformat, this would be simplified to just `var`, which would be incorrect in the context of our project.

Due to the fact that this autoformat can sometimes cause bugs in the formatted program, perhaps we could consider not enabling this autoformat by default?

issue_title: Upload synchronize except where local file has been deleted?
issue_body:
I'm using v2.0.0 to sync files from one computer to an FTP server. Only a history of the last 7 files are kept on the local computer: I have a script that periodically deletes anything older than 7 days. I've found that `UploadSynchronizer` deletes the file on the remote FTP server after the local copy is deleted, which is not the behaviour I want. I'd like to sync local files to the FTP server, but not have to keep the full archive of files on the local computer.

I'm currently using options:
```python
        upload_opts = {"force": True,
                       "resolve": "local",
                       "verbose": 3}
```

I think this is because `pyftpsync` keeps track of files it's seen before, and when it no longer sees the local file I delete, it also deletes it on the remote.

Is there some way to turn off this behaviour? I looked around the code and couldn't see a way - it seems that it's hard-coded in the operations map in `resources.py`?

issue_title: pywin32 for Python 3.7 build broken? datetime calls going awry
issue_body:
I think the pywin32 build provided in pip for Python 3.7.0 is broken - both 32-bit and 64-bit.
In investigating a crash I was getting making a COM call which returned a datetime I found some odd behaviour. In looking at the issues currently raised against pywin32 I initially chimed in on:
https://github.com/mhammond/pywin32/issues/1216
I have since run the same simple test under Python 3.6.6 on the same machine and it worked.
Here's my simple test script:
```import pythoncom
import time

t = time.time()
print("Initial t: %s" % int(t))
try:
	print(pythoncom.MakeTime(t))
except Exception as e:
	print(e)
print("Sleeping 5")
time.sleep(5)
try:
	print(pythoncom.MakeTime(time.time()))
except Exception as e:
	print(e)
```
So first I run it under this version:
```Python 3.7.0 (v3.7.0:1bf9cc5093, Jun 27 2018, 04:06:47) [MSC v.1914 32 bit (Intel)] on win32```
And I get this:
```Initial t: 1534317845
days=1719382832; must have magnitude <= 999999999
Sleeping 5
days=1719382832; must have magnitude <= 999999999
```
That exception actually comes from check_delta_day_range in _datetimemodule.c which is only called from new_delta_ex. The number of days mentioned in the exception log bears no relation to the value passed into MakeTime and doesn't change over time. 

pythoncom_MakeTime calls PyWinMethod_NewTime which calls PyWin_NewTime... From looking through the code that's most likely calling PyWinTimeObject_Fromtime_t and then PyDateTimeAPI->DateTime_FromTimestamp ... my guess is that final call is ending up going to the wrong function in _datetimemodule.c (perhaps to new_delta_ex)

The crash I was investigating, as I noted in my comment on issue 1216, seems to have PyWinObject_FromSYSTEMTIME apparently (according to the stack trace) calling new_date_ex when there's no way it should be. It should be calling PyDateTimeAPI->DateTime_FromDateAndTime - my guess is that call is ending up going to new_date_ex and so the type argument of new_date_ex (a pointer) is getting a number of hours, hence the access violation and crash.

So on that basis it looks like something is broken with how the pywin32 build for python 3.7.0 is calling functions in _datemodule and calls are landing on the wrong function, which is leading to weirdness at best or access violations at worse.

Possibly the same underlying issue with the build is leading to a number of the other issues reported here against 3.7.0. As noted on issue 1216 chrullrich is seeing the same misbehaviour with the 64-bit build that I'm seeing with the 32-bit build. I've seen the same behaviour on two different Windows versions as well with the 32-bit build.

issue_title: Seek method returning incorrect messages on compressed topic when using max_poll_records
issue_body:
While using seek method of `kafka.consumer.group.seek' for a given partition, offset, we are seeing the inconsistent behavior for the messages returned with the subsequent poll method.
The issue is easily reproducible for the given topic (compacted).

Part of Workflow: 
```
from kafka.consumer.group import KafkaConsumer

topic_partition = TopicPartition(topic, 0)
consumer = KafkaConsumer(*consumer_config)
consumer.assign([topic_partition]) 
start_offset = 100 # Example value: highwatermark - 10
consumer.seek(partition=topic_partition, offset=start_offset)
messages = consumer.poll(timeout_ms=1000, max_records=1)[topic_partition]
message = messages[0]
print('Offset found:', message.offset, 'Expected offset:', start_offset)

Sample Output:
$ Offset found:80 Expected offset:100
```


Observation:
* If iterator interface is used instead of poll interface, the issue no longer exists. My guess is somewhere while polling for messages, the fetched offsets are not updated or fetched messages are not skipped. It looks like iterator method is not using fetched_records api that's why it works fine.

* At times it does give correct messages (especially when given offset is closer to highwatermark)

Please let me know if any other details are required.

AND(NOT(Symbol('A')), NOT(OR(NOT(Symbol('A')), NOT(Symbol('B')))))

By adding:

term  = term.literalize()

after

term = self.__class__(*args, eval=False)

to the method DualBase.eval, this fixes the problem:

issue_title: defer_size of type str are not taken into account
issue_body:
When calling `read_file` with `defer_size='512 KB'`, the conversion of `defer_size` to `Integer` is never performed.
On version 0.9.9 with python3, this leads to
```
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dicom/filereader.py", line 614, in read_file
    force=force)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dicom/filereader.py", line 553, in read_partial
    stop_when=stop_when, defer_size=defer_size)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dicom/filereader.py", line 305, in read_dataset
    raw_data_element = next(de_gen)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/site-packages/dicom/filereader.py", line 212, in data_element_generator
    if defer_size is not None and length > defer_size:
TypeError: unorderable types: int() > str()
```
There is no exception in python2 because comparing a `str` to an `Integer` always give the same result :
```
>>> 10000000 > '512 KB'
False
```
Nevertheless, `defer_size` of type `str` are not taken into account in python2.

issue_title: Fixed in PRs #108 and #110 - Key object verification is incomplete and inconsistent
issue_body:
The ECDSA implementation seems to wrap around `0`/`generator_secp256k1.order()`:

```python
>>> from pycoin.ecdsa.secp256k1 import generator_secp256k1
>>> from pycoin.ecdsa import public_pair_for_secret_exponent
>>> pp1 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, -1)
>>> pp2 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, generator_secp256k1.order() - 1)
>>> pp1 == pp2
True
>>> pp1 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, 0)
>>> pp2 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, generator_secp256k1.order() + 0)
>>> pp1 == pp2
True
>>> pp1 == (None, None)
True
>>> pp1 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, 1)
>>> pp2 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, generator_secp256k1.order() + 1)
>>> pp1 == pp2
True
```

In fact:

```python
>>> for i in range(256):
...   pp1 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, i)
...   pp2 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, generator_secp256k1.order() + i)
...   assert pp1 == pp2
...   pp1 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, -i)
...   pp2 = public_pair_for_secret_exponent(generator_secp256k1, generator_secp256k1.order() - i)
...   assert pp1 == pp2
...
>>>
```

This is a little misleading, but something we can probably live with. *EDIT: For clarification, I mean that given a raw random distribution of potential secret exponents [0..2<sup>256</sup>), wrapping around at the order point favors the first 0x14551231950b75fc4402da1732fc9bebf keys by 2:1.<sup>[1]</sup>*

However, there also seems to be a behavioral asymmetry in `pycoin.key.Key` (which is at the core of this issue):

```python
>>> from pycoin.key import Key
>>> k = Key(secret_exponent=0)
>>> k
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "pycoin/key/Key.py", line 222, in __repr__
    r = self.public_copy().as_text()
  File "pycoin/key/Key.py", line 199, in public_copy
    is_compressed=(self._hash160_compressed is not None), netcode=self._netcode)
  File "pycoin/key/Key.py", line 43, in __init__
    raise ValueError("exactly one of secret_exponent, public_pair, hash160 must be passed.")
ValueError: exactly one of secret_exponent, public_pair, hash160 must be passed.
>>> k.public_pair()
>>> k = Key(secret_exponent=generator_secp256k1.order())
>>> k
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "pycoin/key/Key.py", line 222, in __repr__
    r = self.public_copy().as_text()
  File "pycoin/key/Key.py", line 198, in public_copy
    return Key(public_pair=self.public_pair(), prefer_uncompressed=self._prefer_uncompressed,
  File "pycoin/key/Key.py", line 123, in public_pair
    if not ecdsa.is_public_pair_valid(ecdsa.generator_secp256k1, public_pair):
  File "pycoin/ecdsa/ecdsa.py", line 125, in is_public_pair_valid
    return generator.curve().contains_point(public_pair[0], public_pair[1])
  File "pycoin/ecdsa/ellipticcurve.py", line 56, in contains_point
    return ( y * y - ( x * x * x + self.__a * x + self.__b ) ) % self.__p == 0
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'NoneType' and 'NoneType'
>>> k.public_pair()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "pycoin/key/Key.py", line 123, in public_pair
    if not ecdsa.is_public_pair_valid(ecdsa.generator_secp256k1, public_pair):
  File "pycoin/ecdsa/ecdsa.py", line 125, in is_public_pair_valid
    return generator.curve().contains_point(public_pair[0], public_pair[1])
  File "pycoin/ecdsa/ellipticcurve.py", line 56, in contains_point
    return ( y * y - ( x * x * x + self.__a * x + self.__b ) ) % self.__p == 0
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'NoneType' and 'NoneType'
```

First, `Key.__init__` allows silent creation of a corrupt Key object when passed a bad value for `secret_exponent`. Second, if `secret_exponent` is 0, one can extract (without triggering an exception) a `None` public key from the `public_pair()` method. However, if the `secret_exponent` is `generator_secp256k1.order()`, then the `public_pair()` method raises an exception.

I'm pretty sure the correct behavior is that `Key.__init__` should raise an exception in the constructor when `secret_exponent` is bad.

Tagging as possibly related to #103.

--

<sup>[1]</sup> Derviation:

```python
>>> distribution_bits = 2 ** 256 - 1
>>> order_bits = 0xfffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffebaaedce6af48a03bbfd25e8cd0364141 - 1 # sort of; 0 is still rejected
>>> favored_bits = distribution_bits ^ order_bits
>>> '0x{:x}'.format(favored_bits)
u'0x14551231950b75fc4402da1732fc9bebf'
```

This is not an issue where secret exponents not in [1, order - 1] are rejected outright. One problem, is where the secret exponent is derived from a seed (i.e., hidden from the caller, e.g., `BIP32Node.from_master_secret(...)`). This can be checked after-the-fact, however:

```python
>>> k1 = Key(secret_exponent=1)
>>> k2 = Key(secret_exponent=generator_secp256k1.order() + 1)
>>> k1.public_pair() == k2.public_pair()
True
>>> k1.secret_exponent() == k2.secret_exponent()
False
>>> k1 == k2
False
>>> k1.secret_exponent() >= 1 and k1.secret_exponent() < generator_secp256k1.order()
True
>>> k2.secret_exponent() >= 1 and k2.secret_exponent() < generator_secp256k1.order()
False
```

To put this in perspective, consider:

```python
>>> from decimal import Decimal
>>> Decimal(favored_bits + 1) / Decimal(distribution_bits + 1)
Decimal('3.734455345040134152759582548E-39')
```

issue_title: tar extraction fails on Windows
issue_body:
The os-specific separator is not used when extracting tarballs in python. So, on windows, all files are using the '/' separator. So in tar.py, the file extraction/replacement in get_members fails to work properly.
By removing the os.sep from the prefix, the code works cross-platform.
```python
def get_members(tar, prefix):
    for tar_info in tar.getmembers():
        if tar_info.name.startswith(prefix):
            tar_info.name = tar_info.name[len(prefix)+1:] # new +1 here to compensate
            yield tar_info
prefix = command.version #+ os.sep
tar.extractall(self.path, get_members(tar, prefix))
```

I've made this change locally on Windows and Linux and they both work. Maybe you can think of a more elegant solution.

issue_title: `export` should strip the `src` path element
issue_body:
Generally, when working with `vcs`, my working directory contains a subdirectory called `src` and all the actions I take operate on the contents of `src` without me referring to it explicitly.

But when I `vcs export` to capture the state of my source tree, if I do it from above `src`, then the local paths that are produced start with `src/`. If I subsequently `vcs import` that result somewhere else in the normal way, I end up with the checkout going into `src/src/`.

To get what I consider to be the right answer, I have to run `vcs export` inside `src`. It seems to me that running `vcs export` at the top should give me the local paths without the leading `src/` path element.

I'm happy to make a PR to implement the change, but I first want to confirm that I have the right perspective.

issue_title: Automate composite dose processing
issue_body:
**We intend to add automation of composite DVH calculation based on multiple DICOM RT Dose files**

Because not all treatment planning systems can export a composite plan (e.g., putting all rxs in a single rt plan file), functionality to import all rt plan files for a given study instance UID was added (see 3e3db72a39e92b90780ac2a3a7937e33d12b9b7f).

Currently, DVH Analytics will still only import the latest dose and struct files for a given study instance UID, so the user may need to use other software to generate a single rt dose file of the composite dose.  We plan to do this for you in a future update.

In summary, DVH Analytics assumes:
* All rt plan files in the inbox are pertinent (unless IMPORT_LATEST_PLAN_ONLY is set to True in options.py)
* Only the latest rt plan and rt struct files will be used, and are assumed to reflect a composite
     * All DICOM files for a given study instance UID will still be catalogued, even if their data is not added to the Plans, Beams, Rxs, or DVHs tables

issue_title: Make scipy an optional dependency
issue_body:
Currently both numpy and scipy are needed to install imagehash but this only makes sense when you're using the phash function. If you're using average_hash or dhash then you only need numpy.

People can then install this library as needed by using, e.g., `pip install ImageHash` or `pip install ImageHash[pHash]` using the optional dependency functionality.

issue_title: NestedHyperlinkedRelatedField should default use_pk_only_optimization to False
issue_body:
NestedHyperlinkedRelatedField, which inherits from DRF's HyperlinkedRelatedField, defaults to using a [PKOnlyObject](https://github.com/encode/django-rest-framework/blob/ee1a9fcef659d0a5885712575562acb71a502f21/rest_framework/relations.py#L61) optimized object rather than the full model instance if the lookup_field kwarg is `'pk'`. However, this won't work for the nested relationship lookup process because the object literally only has a `pk` field and any attempt to examine other fields results in an exception.

By overriding use_pk_only_optimization() in the field to return False, it guarantees the full objects will be used and then nested lookups will work correctly. I'll submit a PR too.

This code seems to have LaTeX support, however, I can't wrap my mind of the internal working of the process.

cacheback does the following to generate key for the cache:

issue_title: MultiFormView Forms that aren't validated as part of a group in POST should be instantiated as if a GET
issue_body:
Currently all forms are instantiated based on the POST data, whether or not they end get getting validated. This means that when the page is finally rendered they have empty data rather than their initial data, for example a formset will have no form rows rather than showing the real initial data.

So the way forms need to be instantiated AFTER we know what group was POSTed, so we can prevent forms outside of the group being given the data and files. At the moment we use the same approach for instantiation that we do with GET, this may need to change.

issue_title: Fix DesignDocument so that an empty view sub-document is not created
issue_body:
When instantiating a DesignDocument, an empty views sub-document is created by default.  If no view is added to the DesignDocument and that DesignDocument is then created/saved to the remote server, that empty views sub-document is written to the DesignDocument.  This should not be the case.  For example:

```python
with DesignDocument(db, '_design/ddoc001x') as ddoc:
    ddoc['foo'] = 'bar'
print db['_design/ddoc001x']
```

will output:

`{u'_rev': u'1-95205e2e4d80983f11db5f6018adb430', u'_id': u'_design/ddoc001x', u'foo': u'bar', u'views': {}}`

This means that the design document saved to the database contains `'views': {}` which it should not.

- Fix the DesignDocument.save() method to check for an empty views sub-document and to remove it before saving it.
- One thing to keep in mind is that removing the "views" key from the DesignDocument object could cause unwanted side effects and introduce new bugs, so a poosible solution would be to copy the DesignDocument content and update that instead as part of the save() method and then updating the original DesignDocument with the new "_rev"
- This bug is somewhat related to #66 and should be resolved before or as part of that bug fix.

issue_title: avoid exposing "real path" of watch root to the user
issue_body:
Watchman resolves any symlinks (including ancestors) of a watch root to determine if it's already being watched. This is fine, except when a client uses the `watch-list` command to determine if a parent directory is a watched root (which I won't need to do if #599 is implemented). I *could* resolve every ancestor of a given path before validating it, but that seems like extra work that can/should be avoided.

I'm proposing that `watch-list` preserves the path of every watch root as they were received (before any symlink resolution). Internally, you can still resolve the watch root to ensure no duplicate watches.

The real-world example where this came to my attention: `/var/` resolves to `/private/var/` on OSX.

issue_title: KeywordsVocabulary - make keyword_index dynamic
issue_body:
The z3cform AjaxSelectWidget (select2) is able to supply a keyword index field:

```
    <field name="my_field" type="zope.schema.Tuple" lingua:independent="true">
      <description>A keyword query</description>
      <required>False</required>
      <title>Custom Keyword</title>
      <value_type type="zope.schema.Choice">
        <vocabulary>plone.app.vocabularies.Keywords</vocabulary>
        <field>my_field</field>        
        <orderable>True</orderable>
      </value_type>
      <form:widget type="plone.app.z3cform.widget.AjaxSelectFieldWidget" />
    </field>
```

This requires a keyword index "my_field" in the portal catalog. 

It is trivial to implement:

```
    def all_keywords(self, keyword_index, kwfilter):
        site = getSite()
        self.catalog = getToolByName(site, 'portal_catalog', None)
        if self.catalog is None:
            return SimpleVocabulary([])
        try:
            index = self.catalog._catalog.getIndex(keyword_index)
        except KeyError:
            index = self.catalog._catalog.getIndex(self.keyword_index)
            
        return safe_simplevocabulary_from_values(index._index, query=kwfilter)


    def __call__(self, context, query=None):
        section = self.section(context)
        keyword_index = context and context.REQUEST.get('field', self.keyword_index)
        if section is None:
            return self.all_keywords(keyword_index, query)
        return self.keywords_of_section(section, keyword_index, query)
```

issue_title: socket.recv() does't throw exception when interrupted by a signal
issue_body:
Hi,

socket.recv doesn't throw exception when interrupted by a signal since pyzmq version 14.7.0. This makes it impossible or hard to cleanly exit a program by using signal (like SIGINT).

The following script illustrates the bug:
```python
import signal
import zmq

print("Current libzmq version is %s" % zmq.zmq_version())
print("Current pyzmq version is %s" % zmq.__version__)

def signal_handler(signum, frame):
    print("Signal handler called")
    pass

ctx = zmq.Context()
socket = ctx.socket(zmq.REP)
socket.bind("tcp://*:5555")

signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal_handler)
try:
    socket.recv()
except zmq.ZMQError as e:
    print(e)
```

Example run on pyzmq 14.6.0:
```
Current libzmq version is 4.0.4
Current pyzmq version is 14.6.0
^CSignal handler called
Interrupted system call
```

Example (failed) run on pyzmq 14.7.0:
```
Current libzmq version is 4.0.4
Current pyzmq version is 14.7.0
^CSignal handler called
^CSignal handler called
^CSignal handler called
^CSignal handler called
^\Quit
```

jsonpickle.decode(jsonpickle.encode(np.array([0,1])))
```

Yields:

> File "c:\users\hans\appdata\local\programs\python\python36\lib\site-packages\jsonpickle\ext\numpy.py", line 134, in flatten
>     if self.size_treshold >= obj.size or self.size_treshold is None:
>

> 


This needs fixing in two places (lines 134 and 237).

issue_title: Encoding environment variables
issue_body:
The following environment variables influence the default encoding of the Python interpreter:

```sh
$ declare -x|fgrep -i utf
declare -x LANG="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_ADDRESS="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_COLLATE="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_CTYPE="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_IDENTIFICATION="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_MEASUREMENT="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_MESSAGES="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_MONETARY="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_NAME="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_NUMERIC="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_PAPER="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_TELEPHONE="en_US.UTF-8"
declare -x LC_TIME="en_US.UTF-8"
```

These should be unset or set to `C` in the Makefile to "genericize" the encoding environment and simulate a wider range of end-user environments.

Inspired by PR #474

issue_title: Why is configuration file called 'client.cfg'
issue_body:
Maybe a stupid question, but why is the configuration file called `client.cfg`, when it typically contains non-client (e.g. scheduler) stuff as well?

issue_title: Custom date interval behavior confusing
issue_body:
The `Year`, `Week`, `Month` and `Day` date interval types all add one extra day to the end date so that the date interval is inclusive on the beginning date and exclusive on the end date.  This makes a lot of sense and lets me easily write code like this:

```python
class KPIReport(luigi.Task):
    date_interval = luigi.DateIntervalParameter()

    def run(self):
        start = datetime.datetime.combine(self.date_interval.date_a, datetime.time())
        end = datetime.datetime.combine(self.date_interval.date_b, datetime.time())
        num_members = User.find({'signup_time': {'$gte': start, '$lt': end}}) 
        # etc...
```
So now if I run ```python reports.py KPIReport --date-interval 2015-03-20``` everything works out and I get a report for that single day.

However now if I want to run the same report on an arbitrary range, such as the last 7 days, I might think I can simply run ```python reports.py KPIReport --date-interval 2015-03-14-2015-03-20```.  But now instead of ending at 23:59:59 on the 20th my date interval ends at 23:59:59 on the 19th.  

It seems to me that the `Custom` date interval should add one day to the end date just as all of the other implementations do, or is there a different best practice for this scenario?

issue_title: Hive partition display query in table check requires semicolon for later Hive versions.
issue_body:
We recently upgraded our Hive from 2.1.1 to 2.3.2 and we experienced our jobs hanging upon the table partition check. 
After investigating we found the issue to be this query found [https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/luigi/contrib/hive.py#L144](here):

`stdout = run_hive_cmd("""use %s; show partitions %s partition
                                (%s)""" % (database, table, self.partition_spec(partition)))`

It should have a semicolon at the end of the statement. This was not necessary in previous Hive versions but hung in later versions when the semicolon was not present.

issue_title: jsonschema: harmonize document_type Vs. collection
issue_body:
@kaplun commented on [Wed Jun 15 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215)

Currently the `collection` field is just a porting of MARC `980`. E.g.:

``` json
{"collections": [
    {"primary": "CORE"},
    {"primary": "Book"},
    {"primary": "HEP"},
    {"primary": "Citeable"}
]}
```

On the other hand the concept of `document_type` is managed by the enhancer `facet_inspire_doc_type`. E.g.:

``` json
{"facet_inspire_doc_type": ["book"]}
```

This is suboptimal.
- [ ] `Citeable` should become a flag and be added at indexing time based on other values
- [ ] `CORE` should be declared as a flag and be available in all schemas
- [ ] `HEP` is actually redundant since it represents the fact that this is a record from Literature
- [ ] `facet_inspire_doc_type` should become `document_type` and be populated by dojson, rather than enhanced before indexing.

---

@kaplun commented on [Thu Aug 25 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215#issuecomment-242554358)

I think we should bump priority of this one, since category is really scattered around the code base in a wrong way.

---

@jacquerie commented on [Fri Aug 26 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215#issuecomment-242560149)

This needs a spec. The thing I refactored in https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/blob/25cba484c652d21c112628c4967e684c02d6fcfd/inspirehep/modules/records/receivers.py#L120-L210 is a 1 to 1 correspondence with the code that was there before, but makes no sense to me.

You need to define precisely:
- What should we do with `collections`
- What are the allowable `document_types`
- How are the `980__a` values mapped to those allowable values
- What is the algorithm that sets `Citeable`

---

@kaplun commented on [Mon Sep 19 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215#issuecomment-247975517)

> What should we do with collections

Should disappear.

> What are the allowable document_types

Exactly the keys that  you have defined in the two tables in the docstring `populate_inspire_document_type()`.

> How are the 980__a values mapped to those allowable values

Those that are document types are mapped to document types (possibly with the same value as in 980). Those that are flags, such as `citeable` and `core` should be mapped to a corresponding flag. (I think we have it for core already). `deleted` is also mapped to a deleted field.

> What is the algorithm that sets `Citeable`:

Mmh. I guess it's more the question of what is not citeable. I see by default anything that comes from arXiv is citeable. @annetteholtkamp can you help here?

---

@jmartinm commented on [Wed Oct 05 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215#issuecomment-251618614)

Now that https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/pull/1589 is merged, and once we get rid of the `collections` field, note that we will still have a `_collections` field managed by `invenio-collections`.

This field gets populated based on a query matching the record (see [config](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/blob/master/inspirehep/config.py#L150)) so that config will have to be amended for the queries to match the new `document_type` field.

---

@jmartinm commented on [Thu Oct 06 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215#issuecomment-251658158)

Collection fields are:

```
   1100059 HEP 
    832449 Citeable 
    698265 CORE 
    584001 Published 
    401801 arXiv 
    312449 ConferencePaper 
     57480 Arxiv 
     51674  
     26942 Thesis 
     27168 Review 
     10148 Lectures 
      5637 NOTE 
      7727 Proceedings 
      7507 noncore 
      4643 THESIS

      4003 Withdrawn 
      3982 Hep 
      3344 Book 
       172 D0-PRELIMINARY-NOTE 
      2069 BOOK 
      1239 NONCORE 
      1240 PROCEEDINGS 
      1115 citeable 
       891 BookChapter 
       452 Conference 
        33 Core 
        11 REPORT 
         5 Preprint 
         6 published 
         3 core 
         3 Note 
         2 Noncore 
         2 Report 
         1 PUBLISHED

         1 book 
         1 proceedings 
         1 NonCore 
         1 Conferencepaper 
         1 Accelerators

```

Our schema says possible document types are:

```
[
"Published",
 "arXiv",
 "ActivityReport",
 "ConferencePaper",
 "Thesis",
 "Review",
 "Lectures",
 "Note",
 "Proceedings",
 "Introductory",
 "Book",
 "BookChapter",
 "Report"
  ],
```

And our current document type facet has the following mapping (from `980__a` value to facet value):

```
        'published': 'peer reviewed',
        'thesis': 'thesis',
        'book': 'book',
        'bookchapter': 'book chapter',
        'proceedings': 'proceedings',
        'conferencepaper': 'conference paper',
        'note': 'note',
        'report': 'report',
        'activityreport': 'activity report',
        'lectures': 'lectures',
        'review': 'review',

'preprint' if no journal info
```

So for this issue to proceed we would need:
- [x] For each value in `980__a` what document type from our schema to assign.
- [x] Should the document type in the schema be human readable `ActivityReport` vs `Activity Report` for example.
- [ ] Complete the `enum` in our jsonschema to acomodate document types such as the hidden collections: `Hal Hidden`, notes from different experiments and so on.
- [x] Do we still need the receiver to convert the document types into a more 'user facing' facet, with values such as `Preprint` or `Peer Reviewed` which are not mentioned as document types in the schema.

---

@kaplun commented on [Wed Oct 05 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215#issuecomment-251661399)

@jmartinm Thanks. I'd suggest @annetteholtkamp et al. can help us removing all the outliers from 980__a

@kaplun commented on [Thu Oct 06 2016](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/issues/1215#issuecomment-251662505)

> - [X] For each value in 980__a what document type from our schema to assign.

```
1100059 HEP -> Literature Schema
    832449 Citeable -> citeable flag
    698265 CORE -> 'core' flag: True
    584001 Published -> published flag
    401801 arXiv -> ignore (redundant)
    312449 ConferencePaper -> 'conference paper'
     57480 Arxiv ->  ignore (redundant)
     51674  -> ignore (W00t?)
     26942 Thesis -> 'thesis'
     27168 Review -> 'review'
     10148 Lectures -> 'lectures'
      5637 NOTE -> 'note'
      7727 Proceedings -> 'proceedings'
      7507 noncore -> 'core' flag: False
      4643 THESIS -> 'thesis'
      3488 Introductory -> 'introductory'
      4003 Withdrawn -> 'withdrawn' flag
      3982 Hep -> Literature Schema
      3344 Book -> 'book'
       172 D0-PRELIMINARY-NOTE 
       891 BookChapter -> 'book chapter'
       452 Conference -> Wot? In HEP?
        11 REPORT -> 'report'

         6 published -> ignore redundant
```

> - [X] Should the document type in the schema be human readable ActivityReport vs Activity Report for example.

I believe so: anyway cataloguers will edit record either though scripts or through the editor, which will enforce the accepted values. Therefore there is no need to introduce a simplified spelling to avoid typos.

> - [ ] Complete the enum in our jsonschema to acomodate document types such as the hidden collections: Hal Hidden, notes from different experiments and so on.

I will create a dedicated issue for that.

> - [X] Do we still need the receiver to convert the document types into a more 'user facing' facet, with values such as Preprint or Peer Reviewed which are not mentioned as document types in the schema.

issue_title: Document method to disable "Install python-Levenshtein" warning
issue_body:
I'm using FuzzyWuzzy on Google App Engine.

To achieve scale, GAE's Standard Environment restricts low level C implementations. As such, python-Levenshtein cannot be used, but the log files warn:
> UserWarning: Using slow pure-python SequenceMatcher. Install python-Levenshtein to remove this warning

From: [fuzzywuzzy/fuzz.py#L33](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/fuzz.py#L33)

These are generated every time the application is instantiated and, in this case, are unresolvable because python-Levenshtein builds on Levenshtein.c.

@josegonzalez Has previously covered the reasoning for the logging behaviour in:
[67: UserWarning: Using slow pure-python SequenceMatcher](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/issues/67), and
[100: Unnecessary warning should be removed](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/issues/100). In both cases the resolution was "install python-Levenshtein".

Please consider:

- removing the unnecessary warning, or
- downgrading the warning to debug level, or
- providing a method for disabling the warning in the docs.

Regardless of outcome can I note that there is a need/use for a great pure-python fuzzy matching library (Go FuzzyWuzzy!), and therefore python-Levenshtein should always be an option? (Rather than a requirement, as suggested in #67.)

issue_title: v15.1.0 activate still fails in bash strict mode with PS1: unbound variable
issue_body:
It seems that an old bug is still here: `PS1: unbound variable` when called with bash strict mode.

Clearly virtualenv needs a test using bash strict mode and an almost empty set of environment variables, so we avoid future regressions on this.

Here is a simple way to replicate the bug:
```
#!/bin/bash
set -euox pipefail
pip install -U virtualenv
virtualenv xxx
unset PS1
source xxx/bin/activate
```

issue_title: venv/bin/python does not set VIRTUAL_ENV (and PATH) environment
issue_body:
I was surprised to find out that there is a difference between sourcing the bin/activate file (which seems to be only compatible to Bash and Fish) and running bin/python directly.

I expected the latter to work equally well.  It is unfortunately much easier to call a specific python interpreter  than to set up the environment when dealing with multiple virtual environments.

The way I read the current documentation, it is also the expected behaviour that it "just works" when calling scripts inside the virtual env directly. (citing https://virtualenv.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/, emphasis mine)

> 
This will change your $PATH so its first entry is the virtualenv’s bin/ directory. (You have to use source because it changes your shell environment in-place.) This is all it does; it’s purely a convenience. If you **directly run** a script or the python interpreter from the virtualenv’s bin/ directory (e.g. path/to/ENV/bin/pip or /path/to/ENV/bin/python-script.py) there’s **no need for activation**.


This, however, is not completely true. If you `subprocess.call(pip) ` from an activated python, you get different results from calling it from venv/bin/python.  This is probably what lead to https://github.com/fiduswriter/fiduswriter/issues/195. The difference is at least in the `VIRTUAL_ENV` and `PATH` variables. But other people also have this problem, it seems:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11963019/running-python-script-from-inside-virtualenv-bin-is-not-working
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/8052926/running-subprocess-within-different-virtualenv-with-python
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3287038/cron-and-virtualenv

This issue seems to have cropped up here (https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/issues/247) albeit in a different flavour. 

It'd be nice if this could be addressed and a "one-stop" solution be provided (rather than the two-step source and call).

issue_title: count does not work on line subclasses like channels or weirs
issue_body:
When you have an lines model instance and call count on it, it will give you the overall count of flow lines in the grid admin file:
```
from threedigrid.admin.gridadmin import GridH5Admin

f = 'gridadmin.h5'
ga = GridH5Admin(f)
ga.lines.count
```

If you call count on a predefined subset like channels
``` 
ga.lines.channels.count
```
It will yield the same result, thus not applying the filter ``content_type__eq='v2_channel``

issue_title: Add convertion routines between `cartesian`, `xy` and `rc` conventions
issue_body:
## Description
Unfortunatley, there is no global agreement between developers of different image processing tools on the preferable coordinate convention yet. I'm aware of 3 most commonly used ones: `cartesian` (x - horiz. to the right, y - vert. to the top), `xy` (x - horiz. to the right, y - vert. to the bottom), `rc` (r - vert. to the bottom, c - horiz. to the right).
Even current `skimage` algorithms work with two of the above. Things become harder when people start to use several libraries in parallel (i.e. `skimage` + `OpenCV`).

I feel pretty sad reimplementing these routines over and over again for the new projects, and will be really happy to have them in the `skimage` distribution.

Above said, I propose to add to `skimage.util`: `cart_to_xy`, `cart_to_rc`, `xy_to_rc` and corresponding inverse functions, which will operate on list of coordinates and, possibly, images to convert points/image between these coordinate systems.

Please, let me know how you fell about this.

issue_title: Image comparison functions should share a common prefix
issue_body:
Before #1897, we really only had one quantitative image comparison metric: structural similarity, available in `skimage.measure.structural_similarity`. Now we have three more, based on the PSNR, MSE, and NRMSE. 

Each of these directly compares two like-shaped arrays and yields a floating point result, indicating the degree of similarity. **All similar functions should share a prefix, to improve accessibility.**

In #1897 I proposed `compare_*` as such a prefix. Other options were `compare_with_*`, `compare_by_*`, and `metrics_`. I'd also toss in `imcompare_*`, but I dislike the Matlab feel.

As we add more features in the future, I think `metrics_*` is too broad.

issue_title: RFC deprecation policy and utils
issue_body:
# Problem

Currently we are using ``PendingDeprecationWarning`` and ``DeprecationWarning`` to deprecate features in Invenio. E.g.:

```
warnings.warn("Feature X is deprecated. Please use Y instead.", PendingDeprecationWarning)
warnings.warn("Feature X is deprecated. Please use Y instead.", DeprecationWarning)
```

The problem is we do not know easily when we should upgrade from ``PendingDeprecationWarning`` to ``DeprecationWarning`` and when to completely remove the deprecated code.

# Proposal

1. Adopt a deprecation policy. See below.

2. Adopt [utilities similar to Django](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/utils/deprecation.py) for managing deprecations. I.e. add

```
# Current version +0.2
class RemovedInInvenio22Warning(PendingDeprecationWarning):
    pass

# Current version +0.1
class RemovedInInvenio21Warning(DeprecationWarning):
    pass
```

after each release we rename the warnings. This way we get a syntax error for code that has to be removed, and we can easily see when a feature can be removed when.

Suggestion for deprecation policy (which ties into Invenio release process):

A minor release version (A.B) may deprecate certain features from previous releases. If a feature is deprecated in version A.B it will continue to work in version A.B and A.B+1 but raise warnings. It will be completely removed in version A.B+2. Example:

* Invenio 2.0 marks feature X as deprecated. The feature will still work, but it will issue the silent warning ``RemovedInInvenio22Warning``  (PendingDeprecationWarning).
* Invenio 2.1 still contains the feature, but the warning ``RemovedInInvenio22Warning`` is now loud by default (DeprecationWarning).
* Invenio 2.2 will completely remove the feature.

(based on https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.7/internals/release-process/#internal-release-deprecation-policy)

issue_title: workflows: UUID upgrade problem
issue_body:
The change to using UUID type and the following upgrade script introduced in https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/commit/e8f6551d9787ceec99248b2b0ad04b8f22a0d8b4 causes a problem with truncated values for the existing values - essentially corrupting the data (observed locally):
```
>>> Applying workflows_2015_06_05_resize_uuid_columns (Resize uuid columns on workflows tables.)
>>> workflows_2015_06_05_resize_uuid_columns: WARNING: Data truncated for column 'uuid' at row 1
>>> workflows_2015_06_05_resize_uuid_columns: WARNING: Data truncated for column 'uuid' at row 2
...
```
Before the uuid column was `db.String(36)` and a value included hyphens `00011d78-fd76-11e4-b9a6-ecf4bb24`. With the UUID type on MySQL, the hyphens are not considered and the length of the CHAR type is set to 32. During the upgrade the data is still stored with hyphens causing the truncation. 

I noticed this in the output of the upgrade, the corrupt IDs then cause a range of [troubles](https://gist.github.com/anonymous/53c521015b0c34685173) post-upgrade.

I suggest we either (a) change the MySQL type to `CHAR(36)`, or (b) remove hyphens from the data during upgrade.

EDIT: Considering changing a primary key is never a good idea, I suggest (a).

@hachreak @jirikuncar

When accessing the communities page provided by invenio-communities there is an Internal Server Error if user registration is off  ( `SECURITY_REGISTERABLE=False`) and being logged out.

```python
{%- if security.registerable %}
# Sign-up button
{%- endif %}

block around the signup-button. ([see here](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-accounts/blob/559284550f4b6f2fa964f11be27fc269489dbf43/invenio_accounts/templates/invenio_accounts/login_user.html#L49-L52))

issue_title: Factor analysis differs when grouping assets by sector
issue_body:
Analyzing a factor with assets grouped by sector yields different results than analyzing without grouping. 

The following code reproduces the issue:
```
import alphalens
from quantopian.pipeline import Pipeline
from quantopian.research import run_pipeline
from quantopian.pipeline.filters.morningstar import Q1500US
from quantopian.pipeline.classifiers.morningstar import Sector
from quantopian.pipeline.data.morningstar import operation_ratios

MORNINGSTAR_SECTOR_CODES = {
     -1: 'Misc',
    101: 'Basic Materials',
    102: 'Consumer Cyclical',
    103: 'Financial Services',
    104: 'Real Estate',
    205: 'Consumer Defensive',
    206: 'Healthcare',
    207: 'Utilities',
    308: 'Communication Services',
    309: 'Energy',
    310: 'Industrials',
    311: 'Technology' ,    
}

def make_pipeline():
    factor_1 = operation_ratios.operation_margin.latest
    
    universe = (Q1500US() 
                & factor_1.notnull())
    
    pipe = Pipeline(
        columns={
            'factor': factor_1.rank(mask=universe, method='average'),
            'Sector': Sector(mask=universe),
        },
        screen=universe
    )
    return pipe

result = run_pipeline(make_pipeline(), start_date='2013-12-01', end_date='2014-12-01')
result.head()

assets = result.index.levels[1].unique()
pricing = get_pricing(assets, start_date='2013-12-01', end_date='2015-01-01', fields='open_price')

alphalens.tears.create_factor_tear_sheet(factor=result['factor'],
                                         prices=pricing,
                                         quantiles=2,
                                         periods=(1,5,10,30))

factor_data = alphalens.utils.get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns(result['factor'],
                                                                   pricing,
                                                                   groupby=result["Sector"],
                                                                   quantiles=2,
                                                                   periods=(1,5,10,30),
                                                                   groupby_labels=MORNINGSTAR_SECTOR_CODES
                                                                   )

alphalens.tears.create_full_tear_sheet(factor_data, 
                                       group_adjust=True, 
                                       by_group=True)

issue_title: `raven test twisted+https` is broken (event loop isn't started in the test)
issue_body:
As we can see from the source below, an event loop isn't started, and the test passes as successful.

https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/61969ac21d7eda1162cdedd3f066aa8e396fb5ba/raven/scripts/runner.py#L115
David already made a TODO for that :)

IMO, the easiest way to fix it is the following (just a Proof of Concept):
1. Add a method to test the transport to all of the transports (not only for async ones). Some examples:

   * For Twisted transport:
```
@classmethod
def start_test_and_wait(cls, time=3):
    from twisted.internet import reactor
    reactor.callLater(time, reactor.stop)
    reactor.run()
```

   * For a threaded approach (default):

```
@classmethod
def start_test_and_wait(cls, time=3):
    time.sleep(time)
```

* Get a transport for the a client (not sure how to do it, but `client.remote.get_transport()` might helps) and launch the `start_test_and_wait` instead of `time.sleep(3)` in https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/304faee8b1a986deceb7d64084c4df5df45a51e8/raven/scripts/runner.py
* Probably `start_test_and_wait` method should check if the request is successful.

It could save some debugging time for devs like me.

issue_title: Returning files via connexion.send_file or connexion.send_from_directory return an error
issue_body:
### Description
Using connexion.send_file or connexion.send_from_directory return an error. I think this is because they don't return an ResponseContainer object.

### Expected behaviour
File should be sent in response.

### Actual behaviour
File not sent in response, console shows call stack:
```
  Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1982, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1614, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.handle_user_exception(e)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1517, in handle_user_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flask/_compat.py", line 33, in reraise
    raise value
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1612, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.dispatch_request()
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1598, in dispatch_request
    return self.view_functions[rule.endpoint](**req.view_args)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/connexion/decorators/decorator.py", line 118, in wrapper
    response_container = function(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/connexion/decorators/produces.py", line 59, in wrapper
    response = function(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/connexion/decorators/response.py", line 90, in wrapper
    response = function(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/connexion/decorators/decorator.py", line 99, in wrapper
    return self.get_response_container(operation_handler_result)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/connexion/decorators/decorator.py", line 61, in get_response_container
    self.mimetype, response=operation_handler_result)
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/connexion/decorators/decorator.py", line 153, in __init__
    self.data = self._response.get_data()
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/werkzeug/wrappers.py", line 906, in get_data
    self._ensure_sequence()
  File "/home/test/download/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/werkzeug/wrappers.py", line 955, in _ensure_sequence
    raise RuntimeError('Attempted implicit sequence conversion '
RuntimeError: Attempted implicit sequence conversion but the response object is in direct passthrough mode.
```

### Steps to reproduce
```
# setup venv
python3 -m venv --clear venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip3 install --upgrade pip
pip3 install connexion

# manually update swagger_spec_validator until its fixed.
pip3 install --upgrade https://github.com/Yelp/swagger_spec_validator/archive/v2.0.3.zip#egg=swagger_spec_validator-2.0.3

# add swagger.yaml + app.py to current directory.

# create file to upload
mkdir uploads
dd if=/dev/urandom of=uploads/test.bin bs=1024 count=100

# start app.py
python app.py

# download file
curl http://localhost:8000/test -v -o test_rsp.bin
```

swagger.yaml
```
swagger: '2.0'

info:
  version: "0.1.0"
  title: Download Test

schemes:
 - http
consumes:
 - multipart/form-data
produces:
 - application/octet-stream

paths:
  /test:
    get:
      description: download file.
      operationId: "app.test_get"
      responses:
        200:
          description: OK
          schema:
            type: file
```

app.py
```
import connexion
from connexion.decorators.decorator import ResponseContainer

def test_get():
    resp = connexion.send_from_directory('uploads', 'test.bin',
         as_attachment=True, 
         mimetype='application/octet-stream',
         attachment_filename='test_rsp.bin'
    )
    return resp

def main():
    app = connexion.App(__name__, specification_dir='./swagger/')
    app.add_api('swagger.yaml', strict_validation=True, validate_responses=True)
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=8000, threaded=True)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

```

### Additional info:

Output of the commands:
- `python --version`== Python 3.5.2
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"` == Version: 1.1.4
- `pip list` ==
```appdirs (1.4.0)
click (6.7)
clickclick (1.2.1)
connexion (1.1.4)
Flask (0.12)
itsdangerous (0.24)
Jinja2 (2.9.5)
jsonschema (2.6.0)
MarkupSafe (0.23)
packaging (16.8)
pip (9.0.1)
pkg-resources (0.0.0)
pyparsing (2.1.10)
PyYAML (3.12)
requests (2.13.0)
setuptools (34.1.1)
six (1.10.0)
strict-rfc3339 (0.7)
swagger-spec-validator (2.0.3)
Werkzeug (0.11.15)
```

### Workaround
Wrapping it in a ResponseContainer seems to allow it to pass through Connexion but it disables direct passthrough mode set by send_from_directory().

```
def test_get():
    resp = connexion.send_from_directory('uploads', 'test.bin',
         as_attachment=True, 
         mimetype='application/octet-stream',
         attachment_filename='test_rsp.bin'
    )

    return ResponseContainer(
        mimetype=resp.mimetype,
        data=resp.response,
        status_code=200,
        headers=resp.headers
    )
```

issue_title: datetime64[ns] dtype loses hours, minutes, seconds when uploaded to CARTO
issue_body:
I have a dataset of earthquakes from USGS, and I make sure that the data type for the time column is `datetime64[ns]` by using `pd.to_datetime()` and checking the dtypes of the DataFrame. Upon writing the dataset to CARTO, I notice that the hours, minutes, and seconds go missing... hm... 🤔 

In the Jupyter notebook, everything looks good:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12551177/32519136-bdcfec22-c3d9-11e7-83f7-93dea14138ba.png)

After writing to CARTO, time information goes missing:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12551177/32519139-c650b7be-c3d9-11e7-82a1-96e50c2c7c30.png)

Reading the dataset back into Juypter notebook, the time information is still missing:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12551177/32519226-08c59006-c3da-11e7-88eb-1d7b42b6693a.png)

This might just be a CARTO upload problem, but maybe there's something we can do to fix the data type conversion.

issue_title: Datastore: _Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE
issue_body:
We see this fairly often on `commit` with google-cloud-datastore version `0.20`. I believe these should either be retried with exponential backoff automatically by the library (according to [this](https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/errors)) or a more specific error should be raised so user code can deal w/ it (preferably one exception for every one of the cases listed on that doc).

```
_Rendezvous: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE, {"created":"@1476898717.596308747","description":"Secure read failed","file":"src/core/lib/security/transport/secure_endpoint.c","file_line":157,"grpc_status":14,"referenced_errors":[{"created":"@1476898717.596257572","description":"EOF","file":"src/core/lib/iomgr/tcp_posix.c","file_line":235}]})>
```

Edit:

Here's our current (somewhat tested) workaround for this issue in our internal ORM:

``` python


# The maximum number of retries that should be done per Datastore
# error code.
_MAX_RETRIES_BY_CODE = {
    grpc.StatusCode.INTERNAL: 1,
    grpc.StatusCode.ABORTED: 5,  # Only retried for non-transactional commits
    grpc.StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE: 5,
    grpc.StatusCode.DEADLINE_EXCEEDED: 5,
}


def _handle_errors(f, transactional=False):
    @functools.wraps(f)
    def handler(*args, **kwargs):
        retries = 0
        while True:
            try:
                return f(*args, **kwargs)
            # TODO: Replace w/ concrete error types once/if they are
            # added to gcloud.  See: google-cloud-python/issues/2583
            except google.cloud.exceptions._Rendezvous as e:
                code = e.code()
                max_retries = _MAX_RETRIES_BY_CODE.get(code)
                if max_retries is None or transactional and code == grpc.StatusCode.ABORTED:
                    raise

                if retries > max_retries:
                    raise RetriesExceeded(e)

                backoff = min(0.0625 * 2 ** retries, 1.0)
                bread.get_logger().debug("Sleeping for %r before retrying failed request...", backoff)

                retries += 1
                time.sleep(backoff)

    return handler


class Client(datastore.Client):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super(Client, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        self.delete_multi = _handle_errors(self.delete_multi)
        self.get_multi = _handle_errors(self.get_multi)
        self.put_multi = _handle_errors(self.put_multi)

    def transaction(self, *args, **kwargs):
        transaction = super(Client, self).transaction(*args, **kwargs)
        transaction.commit = _handle_errors(transaction.commit, transactional=True)
        return transaction

    def query(self, *args, **kwargs):
        query = super(Client, self).query(*args, **kwargs)
        query.fetch = _handle_errors(query.fetch)
        return query

```

issue_title: No way to override default deadlines in Spanner API
issue_body:
Currently none of the Spanner APIs expose a way to override default deadline. This is especially important for read and query apis since people can use those APIs to stream millions of rows and the default deadline (which I think is [30 seconds](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/spanner/v1/spanner_gapic.yaml#L109)) is just too small for those cases.
One way of doing this would be to allow users to pass in gax CallOptions.

issue_title: Implement "compiling" of the execution engine
issue_body:
These 2 methods are now invalidated:
  https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/engine/engine.py#L19
  https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/goal/phase.py#L61

They attempt to assemble the graph of goals ahead of engine creation in order to populate all the needed options.

In order to setup the flags correctly and have them available for Task execution, the engine needs to be split into 2 phases, compile -> executor then executor.execute().
So essentially there is going to be a compile step = `setup_parser()` / `prepare()`. 
This would require for `prepare()` to be a classmethod - and when `prepare()` and `product_type()` are classmethods, the walk to learn the Task dependency graph will be static. In fact, in the future there is no need for these to be 2 separate methods (`setup_parser` and `prepare`) and can be combined into one. 

With this change, we can "compile" the execution engine for a given sequence of goals just once and re-use it across pants runs.

finagle/finagle-thrift/src/main/java/com/twitter/finagle/thrift/thrift/UpgradeReply.java:167
```

I'm not sure this is coming from zinc, but these are _literal_ paths being used to try to access files in the filesystem. They contain the `:NNN` in the path.

I would imagine that if you use `dtruss` or `strace` (or perhaps `fs_usage`) you could see these for yourself. There's also the neat little project [cluefs](https://github.com/airnandez/cluefs) that performs a similar kind of functionality, only it's cross platform (it's based on FUSE so it's a little slower than native methods, but it seems to work quite well).

issue_title: Package with AWS AppSync schema definition leads to deploy failure
issue_body:
**Description:**

When defining an AWS AppSync GraphQL schema in CloudFormation, there is an option to embed the definition (`Definition` property) in the template or load from S3 (`DefinitionS3Location` property). Embedding the definition in a SAM template leads to inclusion of `DefinitionS3Location` in the packaged template, an error condition for the CloudFormation engine.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. Create SAM template with an AppSync GraphQL schema and serverless function as in the example below.
2. Package.
3. Note that resulting template (e.g. packaged.yaml) includes both a `Definition` and `DefinitionS3Location` property in the `AWS::AppSync::GraphQLSchema` resource.
4. Deploy.
5. CloudFormation will error: "Cannot set both the Definition and the DefinitionS3Location."

**Observed result:**

CloudFormation error: "Cannot set both the Definition and the DefinitionS3Location."

**Expected result:**

Successful deployment.

**Example: template.yaml**

``` yaml
---
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: 'AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31'

Resources:
  MyAppSyncApi:
    Type: AWS::AppSync::GraphQLApi
    Properties:
      AuthenticationType: API_KEY
      Name: my-api

  SessionManagerSchema:
    Type: AWS::AppSync::GraphQLSchema
    Description: Session Manager GraphQL schema
    Properties:
      ApiId: !GetAtt MyAppSyncApi.ApiId
      Definition: |
        type User {
          username: String!
        }
       type Query {
         allUsers(nextToken: String): [User]
       }
      schema {
        query: Query
      }

  MyFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: index.handler
      Runtime: nodejs8.10
```

issue_title: Consider replacing gevent with eventlet
issue_body:
As reported in #176, gevent does not work with Python 2.7.9 (now the default shipped with Ubuntu Vivid) and appears to be unmaintained. The last release of gevent was 2014-04-30 and the last commit to the repo was  2014-12-13. The [relevant bug](https://github.com/gevent/gevent/issues/477) was filed on 2014-09-12 and still hasn't been addressed.

By contrast, eventlet fixed this issue in 0.16, released on 2014-12-30 and continues to be in active development.

If you think this is a viable option I'd be happy to try to create a PR for it. The two libs are fairly similar so I'm hoping it won't be too much work.

issue_title: Invalid symlinks now break utils.create_archive(), previously were skipped, skipped by docker CLI
issue_body:
Version 2.7.0 seems to die if you try to include a symlink that points to something that doesn't exist.  This problem seems to have been introduced in this commit:
 https://github.com/docker/docker-py/commit/5c5705045be72530091a51372ae920f958192bfb

Example from 2.6.1 and `docker` cli tool:
```
$ mkdir test
$ cd test
$ ln -s /doesnt_exist doesnt_exist
$ cat << EOF > Dockerfile 
FROM scratch

COPY . /tmp
EOF
$ docker build -t test . 
Sending build context to Docker daemon   2.56kB
Step 1/2 : FROM scratch
 ---> 
Step 2/2 : COPY . /tmp
 ---> cb6f16dc4b1f
Removing intermediate container 4eda4dd7d7c4
Successfully built cb6f16dc4b1f
Successfully tagged test:latest
$ python
Python 2.7.6 (default, Nov 23 2017, 15:49:48) 
[GCC 4.8.4] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import docker
>>> docker.__version__
'2.6.1'
>>> docker.utils.create_archive(".", ['doesnt_exist'])
<open file '<fdopen>', mode 'w+b' at 0x7fa47208f8a0>
>>> 
```

But as of 2.7.0:
```
$ python
Python 2.7.6 (default, Nov 23 2017, 15:49:48) 
[GCC 4.8.4] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import docker
>>> docker.__version__
'2.7.0'
>>> docker.utils.create_archive(".", ['doesnt_exist'])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/docker/utils/utils.py", line 103, in create_archive
    'Can not access file in context: {}'.format(full_path)
IOError: Can not access file in context: ./doesnt_exist
>>> 
```

It seems that symlinks should be skipped rather than trying to checked to see if they are accessible, as was the previous behavior and the `docker` CLI tool behavior.

# conftest.py

    """Take a pytest fixture request and set up a postgres server for it"""
    test_server = PostgresServer()

    _getconn = mock.patch('app.connection_pool.getconn,
                          side_effect=lambda: test_server.connect('db_name'))
    _putconn = mock.patch('app.connection_pool.putconn',

    return test_server

class PostgresServer(TestServer):

    def kill(self, retries=5):

            os.kill(self.pid, self.kill_signal)

    def pre_setup(self):
        (self.workspace / 'db').mkdir()  # pylint: disable=no-value-for-parameter

        self.pg_bin = subprocess.check_output(["pg_config", "--bindir"]).rstrip()

        ])
        logger.debug("pg_bin = %s", self.pg_bin)

        self.connection_config = {

        self.delete = True

    @property

        return [

            '-F',
            '-k', str(self.workspace / 'db'),
            '-D', str(self.workspace / 'db'),
            '-p', str(self.port),
            # '-c', "log_destination=stderr"
        ]

    def check_server_up(self):
        try:
            print("Connecting to Postgres at localhost:{}".format(self.port))
            with self.connect('postgres') as conn:
                conn.set_session(autocommit=True)
                with conn.cursor() as cursor:
                    cursor.execute("CREATE DATABASE db_name")
            self.connection = self.connect('db_name')
            with open(self.workspace / 'db' / 'postmaster.pid', 'r') as f:
                self.pid = int(f.readline().rstrip())
            return True
        except OperationalError as e:
            logger.warn("Could not connect to test postgres: %s", str(e))
        return False

    def connect(self, database):
        return psycopg2.connect(database=database, **self.connection_config)
```

issue_title: Python 2.6 is being abandoned
issue_body:
Python 2.6 support is being dropped from openstack. As of Juno, openstack requires python 2.7. We're still trying to support Icehouse, which could optionally run with 2.6.

The first problem we've encountered is a dictionary comprehension is oslo messaging, which causes our `py26` tests to fail. Oslo messaging is no longer tested against python 2.6. https://github.com/openstack/oslo.messaging/commit/2734fc8f505ca07ff1a4f4a420807e5c1b4ed812

We need to set upper bounds on the `requirements.txt` and `testrequirements.txt` for python 2.6. We can change the requirements for `py26` tests in `tox.ini`. I don't know how we can change the requirements for the library for python 2.6,

issue_title: Feature: Allow System & Application monitoring based on Graphite
issue_body:
Warning: I have the feeling there should already be a bug report about this (maybe even commented on by me?) but I couldn't find it (I only looked at open bug reports though...)

As a Deis System Administrator I want to see

* Global (VM) Performance statistics over time (memory, CPU Utilization, Network Usage, (free) disk space). Normally this can be done by any networking system (zabbix), but "auto" configuration should be central (eg adding a node should automatically use this system).
Bonus points if I can add alerts that trigger on certain tresholds..

As a Deis user
* Per container metrics. This includes Memory Usage, CPU Usage, Network Usage etc per container & also averages per container type (web, worker, cmd, ...).
* Per web metrics. On top of that for the web type containers I also want metrics on requests (number of requests, response times, number of failed requests)...
This is similar to what Heroku provides. (https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/metrics)
* As bonus points I should be able to use the same toolkit for custom metrics (number of logins, ...)

I think graphite together with collectd / statsd and others will be a perfect method to implement this.

Obviously all of this would probably be implemented as optional components that can just run onto the coreos cluster...


Note: I Understand it is not in Deis' scope to write a new monitoring tool that implements storing metrics, triggers etc... I merely propose using existing tools, whereby I think Graphite based stack might be a good start (althoug something else can probably work as well).


If there is any intrest in this I might have a look at it mystelf to build out a basic platform...

issue_title: config:set k=v with non-validating SSL doesn't work
issue_body:
I upgraded the cluster to v1.5.2 and I'm having trouble getting config:set to work. Previously I have set config successfully, but now I've enabled force https after getting the bugfix for that.

Unfortunately I didn't want to pay for a star-cert this time, so the cert is for the top-domain, not deis.topdomain.com. Still, it's better than no security and besides the domain, the cert is fine.

So I do `ssl_verify: false` in `~/.deis/client.json`.

Now I try to set a config setting:

```
$ deis config:set S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=use-your-own-key
Creating config... /var/folders/j7/wbmqw81j2_jcfwn6_hh0ltlh4zjfkz/T/_MEI3XynoY/requests/packages/urllib3/connectionpool.py:734: InsecureRequestWarning: Unverified HTTPS request is being made. Adding certificate verification is strongly advised. See: https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html
200 OK
{u'updated': u'2015-04-01T16:06:38UTC', u'uuid': u'5cf31ce1-1130-4fac-97e6-2532428b16c4', u'tags': {}, u'app': u'app-com', u'created': u'2015-04-01T16:06:38UTC', u'values': { cut to save the innocent }, u'memory': {}, u'owner': u'haf', u'cpu': {}}
```

Now that's strange. Nor does `config:list` doesn't show the new config.

I had a look at the [code](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/v1.5.2/client/deis.py#L1169-L1185):

``` python
        body = {'values': json.dumps(values)}
        sys.stdout.write('Creating config... ')
        sys.stdout.flush()
        try:
            progress = TextProgress()
            progress.start()
            response = self._dispatch('post', "/v1/apps/{}/config".format(app), json.dumps(body))
        finally:
            progress.cancel()
            progress.join()
        if response.status_code == requests.codes.created:
            version = response.headers['x-deis-release']
            self._logger.info("done, v{}\n".format(version)) # this isn't printed
            config = response.json()
            values = config['values']
            self._logger.info("=== {}".format(app)) # this is printed
            items = values.items()
```

But I can't seem to find how that can go wrong. Does the invalid cert-warning interfere with the POST somehow? In that case, it's probably inside the `self._dispatch` method or children.

As a side-issue; perhaps you could make the ssl_verify non-binary? To accept other well-defined (configured) Host headers, but not accept other issues: self-signed, no cert-chain, invalid cert-chain, cert-chain references cert not in root store, SHA1-hashes used instead of >=SHA2, OCSP failure, CRL failure, invalid signatures, invalid handshakes, client-server handshake algorithm mismatch and so on. 

The scary-looking error message above should really show if I'm using *no encryption*, both on 'login' and usage of the app.

I have verified it's a TLS-client issue by removing security and posting the passwords in plain text over the internets, and then Deis accepts them without complaints. NSA shall be happy to know this ;)
